title: Jennie Faulding Taylor

infobox.value: (6 October 1843 – 31 July 1904), was a British [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") to [China](/wiki/China "China") with the [China Inland Mission](/wiki/China_Inland_Mission "China Inland Mission"). She pioneered the work of single women missionaries in China and eventually married the founder of the mission, [James Hudson Taylor](/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor "James Hudson Taylor"), after the death of his first wife, [Maria Jane Dyer](/wiki/Maria_Jane_Dyer "Maria Jane Dyer"). As Taylor's wife, she assumed many roles within the mission agency when Taylor was overseas - acting at times as a home director for the mission. She encouraged women, both married and unmarried, to participate in the work of the China Inland Mission in ways that had previously only been reserved for male missionaries.

infobox.value: * Born in [England](/wiki/England "England") to William Joseph and Harriet Faulding
* Sailed to [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") on furlough with James Hudson Taylor aboard the MM Volga on 5 August 1871 in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China
* Sailed to China aboard the MM Tigre with Hudson Taylor, 9 October 1872 in Marseille, France (via Paris from London)
* Sailed to England on furlough with Hudson Taylor, 30 August 1874
* Sailed to China without Hudson for famine relief work 2 May 1878
* Retired with Hudson Taylor, after 19 June 1900 in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), Switzerland

first_paragraph: **Jane Elizabeth "Jennie" Faulding Taylor** (6 October 1843 – 31 July 1904), was a British [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") to [China](/wiki/China "China") with the [China Inland Mission](/wiki/China_Inland_Mission "China Inland Mission"). She pioneered the work of single women missionaries in China and eventually married the founder of the mission, [James Hudson Taylor](/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor "James Hudson Taylor"), after the death of his first wife, [Maria Jane Dyer](/wiki/Maria_Jane_Dyer "Maria Jane Dyer"). As Taylor's wife, she assumed many roles within the mission agency when Taylor was overseas - acting at times as a home director for the mission. She encouraged women, both married and unmarried, to participate in the work of the China Inland Mission in ways that had previously only been reserved for male missionaries.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

| Jane Elizabeth Faulding | |
| --- | --- |
| Jennie Taylor | |
| Born | 6 October 1843  England |
| Died | 31 July 1904(1904-07-31) (aged 60)  [Les Chevalleyres](/wiki/Blonay_%E2%80%93_Saint-L%C3%A9gier "Blonay – Saint-Légier"), Switzerland |

**Jane Elizabeth "Jennie" Faulding Taylor** (6 October 1843 – 31 July 1904), was a British [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") to [China](/wiki/China "China") with the [China Inland Mission](/wiki/China_Inland_Mission "China Inland Mission"). She pioneered the work of single women missionaries in China and eventually married the founder of the mission, [James Hudson Taylor](/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor "James Hudson Taylor"), after the death of his first wife, [Maria Jane Dyer](/wiki/Maria_Jane_Dyer "Maria Jane Dyer"). As Taylor's wife, she assumed many roles within the mission agency when Taylor was overseas - acting at times as a home director for the mission. She encouraged women, both married and unmarried, to participate in the work of the China Inland Mission in ways that had previously only been reserved for male missionaries.

## Early life in London

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jennie_Faulding_Taylor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life in London")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/JennieFaulding1866.jpg/250px-JennieFaulding1866.jpg)](/wiki/File:JennieFaulding1866.jpg)

Jennie Faulding in 1866

Jane Elizabeth Faulding was the daughter of a piano manufacturer in London. She was an 1865 graduate of the [Home and Colonial Training College](/w/index.php?title=Home_and_Colonial_Training_College&action=edit&redlink=1 "Home and Colonial Training College (page does not exist)") along with her friend, [Emily Blatchley](/wiki/Emily_Blatchley "Emily Blatchley"). She had met Taylor when she was nine and attended the weekly prayer meeting at the home of Hudson and Maria Taylor in the [East End of London](/wiki/East_End_of_London "East End of London") in 1865; she helped to proofread the Taylors' book "[China's Spiritual Need and Claims](/wiki/China%27s_Spiritual_Need_and_Claims "China's Spiritual Need and Claims")",[[1]](#cite_note-BDCC-1) and was influenced by this work which spoke of the desperate need for the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") message to be brought to the Chinese before they died "without God and without hope in the world".

## The youngest missionary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jennie_Faulding_Taylor&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The youngest missionary")]

When the Taylors were recruiting missionaries to go with them back to China, Faulding and Blatchley volunteered to accompany the 14 other candidates who were all as inexperienced as themselves. Faulding was the junior member[[2]](#cite_note-2) of the [Lammermuir Party](/wiki/Lammermuir_Party "Lammermuir Party"), the largest party of [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") missionaries ever to sail to China in 1866, but she quickly proved herself useful.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Hudson_Taylor_and_the_China_Inland_Mission_-_the_growth_of_a_work_of_God_%281918%29_%2814761904716%29.jpg/250px-Hudson_Taylor_and_the_China_Inland_Mission_-_the_growth_of_a_work_of_God_%281918%29_%2814761904716%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hudson_Taylor_and_the_China_Inland_Mission_-_the_growth_of_a_work_of_God_(1918)_(14761904716).jpg)

Jennie Taylor (née Faulding)

## Pioneering work among women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jennie_Faulding_Taylor&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pioneering work among women")]

On the journey, they weathered two typhoons and a near shipwreck.[[3]](#cite_note-HCF-3) Once in China, they donned Chinese clothes and ventured down the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(China) "Grand Canal (China)"), looking for a place to settle down to mission work. It caused a scandal among the other Westerners in China to see a young single woman like Faulding adopt the Chinese dress,[[1]](#cite_note-BDCC-1) which was considered a compromise with an idolatrous culture. However, Taylor was undeterred in encouraging his missionaries to "adopt all things not sinful that were Chinese in order to save some".

In [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), Faulding proved the value of being an unmarried female, as her daily walks around the neighborhood gave her opportunities to be invited in by the Chinese women, who did not feel threatened as they might have by a foreign man. She met with many local women and established a school in Hangzhou.[[3]](#cite_note-HCF-3)

During her time in China, she lived and worked with Hudson Taylor's wife, Maria, who taught her the Chinese language.[[1]](#cite_note-BDCC-1) Maria died in 1870.

After she had been in China for five years, Faulding was given a [furlough](/wiki/Furlough "Furlough") at the request of her parents. Taylor accompanied her home in 1871. She had keenly felt the loss of Maria Taylor, her friend and mentor, the year previously. On the way back to England, Hudson proposed marriage. She accepted on the condition of her parents' approval, which was not easily obtained. In November of the same year they were married.[[3]](#cite_note-HCF-3) She became the stepmother to Taylor's four surviving children and a successor to Maria as the "Mother of the Mission". Together, they had two children of their own and adopted Millie, an orphaned daughter of a missionary.[[1]](#cite_note-BDCC-1)

## Famine relief and later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jennie_Faulding_Taylor&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Famine relief and later years")]

The news of the terrible [Great North China Famine](/wiki/Famine#China "Famine") of 1877–78 in [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi") Province motivated Faulding to go there with two single women as part of a relief team - when no men could be spared to accompany them on their journey and her husband could not go himself. She began an orphanage in Taiyuan, and distributed aid to the starving people there. She also worked as assistant editor to the quarterly journal *China's Millions*.[[1]](#cite_note-BDCC-1)

Faulding worked alongside her husband until the end of her life. They traveled across the globe many times recruiting missionaries and visiting mission stations in China. She died of breast cancer in [Les Chevalleyres](/wiki/Blonay_%E2%80%93_Saint-L%C3%A9gier "Blonay – Saint-Légier"), Switzerland in 1904.[[4]](#cite_note-GB-4) Hudson remained with her at the end of her life.

## Chronology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jennie_Faulding_Taylor&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Chronology")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **is in [list](/wiki/MOS:LIST "MOS:LIST") format but may read better as [prose](/wiki/MOS:PROSE "MOS:PROSE")**. You can help by [converting this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jennie_Faulding_Taylor&action=edit), if appropriate. [Editing help](/wiki/Help:Editing "Help:Editing") is available. *(February 2024)* |

**Birth to First Time in China 1866**

* Born in [England](/wiki/England "England") to William Joseph and Harriet Faulding
* Sailed to [China](/wiki/China "China") (via the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope")) aboard the Lammermuir with [James Hudson Taylor](/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor "James Hudson Taylor") 26 May 1866 from East India Docks, [London](/wiki/London "London"), England
* Arrived in China aboard the Lammermuir 29 September 1866 in Shanghai, China
* Settled with the Lammermuir party December 1866 in 1 Xin Kai Long (New Lane), [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"), China

**Furlough and marriage**

* Sailed to [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") on furlough with James Hudson Taylor aboard the MM Volga on 5 August 1871 in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China
* Sailed to [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille") via [Saigon](/wiki/Saigon "Saigon"), [Ceylon](/wiki/Ceylon "Ceylon"), [Aden](/wiki/Aden "Aden"), [Suez](/wiki/Suez "Suez") aboard the MM Ava after 5 August 1871 in Guangzhou, [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), China
* Arrived in England 25 September 1871 from Marseille, [France](/wiki/France "France") (via [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") to London)
* Married to James Hudson Taylor, 28 November 1871 in [Regent's Park](/wiki/Regent%27s_Park "Regent's Park") Chapel, [London](/wiki/London "London"), England
* Moved 15 January 1872 to 6 Pyrland Road, [Islington](/wiki/Islington "Islington"), London, England

**Return to China**

* Sailed to China aboard the MM Tigre with Hudson Taylor, 9 October 1872 in Marseille, France (via Paris from London)
* MM Tigre arrived 28 November 1872 in Shanghai, China
* Twins (son and daughter) born on 13 April 1873 in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), [Jiangsu](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), China (stillborn)[[4]](#cite_note-GB-4)

**Raising a family in England**

* Sailed to England on furlough with Hudson Taylor, 30 August 1874
* Arrived 15 October 1874, England
* "Dai Cun-xin" Ernest Hamilton Taylor born 7 January 1875, 2 Pyrland Road, Islington, London
* Amy H. Taylor born 7 April 1876, Islington, London
* Mary Jane Bowyer Duncan adopted, before 25 December 1877, England

**Pioneering work in China**

* Sailed to China without Hudson for famine relief work 2 May 1878
* Led the advance of women missionaries to the far interior about September 1878 in Shanxi, China
* Arrival and reunion with Hudson Taylor, 8 May 1879 in [Yantai](/wiki/Yantai "Yantai") (Cheefoo), [Shandong](/wiki/Shandong "Shandong"), China
* Sailed to England 13 October 1880
* Reunion with Hudson 21 December 1890 in Shanghai, China
* Arrived with Hudson Taylor, March 1892 in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia, Canada
* Sailed to England via Canada, 10 May 1892
* Arrived, 26 July 1892 in England
* Sailed to China via US aboard the [RMS *Germanic*](/w/index.php?title=RMS_Germanic&action=edit&redlink=1 "RMS Germanic (page does not exist)") with Hudson Taylor, 14 February 1894 from [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") & Queenstown, England
* Arrived aboard the RMS *Germanic*, 24 February 1894 in [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island"), New York
* Arrived with Hudson Taylor, 17 April 1894 in Shanghai, China
* Sailed to England after July 1894
* Left China for [India](/wiki/India "India") with Hudson Taylor, February 1896
* Returned to China with Hudson Taylor, April 1896
* Sailed to Italy aboard the Oceania (MM Oceanien) with Hudson Taylor, 2 May 1896
* Arrived at [Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi "Brindisi"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") and visited [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") en route to England, before 17 June 1896
* Arrived in England on 17 June 1896
* Visited with Hudson Taylor in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") c. July 1897
* Sailed to US with Hudson Taylor, 24 November 1897
* Arrived, 18 December 1897 in the United States
* Arrived, 15 January 1898 in Shanghai, China
* Conference with Hudson Taylor, 16 January 1899 in [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"), China
* Sailed to [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") with Hudson Taylor, 25 September 1899
* Sailed to [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") from Australia with Hudson Taylor, 5 January 1900
* Sailed to US from New Zealand with Hudson Taylor, 20 March 1900
* Arrived, 5 April 1900 in [San Francisco, California](/wiki/San_Francisco,_California "San Francisco, California"), United States
* Sailed to England from US, 9 June 1900
* Arrived, 19 June 1900 in England

**Final years**

* Retired with Hudson Taylor, after 19 June 1900 in [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos"), Switzerland
* Died 31 July 1904, aged 60, in Les Chevalleyres, Switzerland
* Buried after 31 July 1904 at La Chiesaz church cemetery, near [Vevey](/wiki/Vevey "Vevey"), Switzerland
